clinical_coherence.notes: 31M right-handed Caucasian math teacher with faciobrachial dystonic seizures (FBDS), progressive cognitive decline, personality change, and hyponatremia - presenting as 'refractory temporal lobe epilepsy.' The clinical picture is brilliantly constructed: FBDS are pathognomonic for LGI1 encephalitis but were initially misdiagnosed as focal motor seizures. Progressive cognitive decline in a math teacher unable to do serial 7s is a powerful clinical detail. Weight loss, personality change, and sleep disturbance add systemic features.

tool_output_consistency.notes: MRI shows bilateral hippocampal SWELLING (not atrophy) - the key imaging distinction from MTS. EEG shows bilateral temporal pathology with very brief (2-3 second) seizures atypical for standard TLE. Labs show Na 126 (SIADH clue). FDG-PET shows temporal hypermetabolism (opposite of epilepsy hypometabolism). LGI1 antibodies positive in serum and CSF. CSF is notably bland (normal cell count), characteristic of LGI1. All tool outputs are internally consistent and diagnostically coherent.

ground_truth_quality.notes: ICD G04.81 correct for autoimmune encephalitis. Primary diagnosis of LGI1 encephalitis is accurate. Critical actions correctly prioritize autoimmune antibody panel, immunotherapy, and AED taper. Contraindicated actions (adding more AEDs, pursuing epilepsy surgery without checking antibodies) are clinically important. Red herrings are excellent.

difficulty_calibration.notes: Exceptional puzzle: initially presented as 'refractory TLE' with temporal EEG abnormalities and hippocampal MRI changes. Requires recognition of FBDS, hippocampal swelling vs sclerosis distinction, significance of hyponatremia, and PET hypermetabolism vs hypometabolism. Agent confidence 0.35 is appropriate.

demographic_plausibility.notes: 31M is within the typical age range for LGI1 encephalitis (though median age is typically 60s, younger cases occur). The occupation as a math teacher provides a powerful cognitive baseline for demonstrating decline.

red_herring_quality.notes: Three excellently designed red herrings: (1) temporal sharp waves mimicking TLE, (2) hippocampal signal abnormality initially read as possible MTS, (3) therapeutic carbamazepine level suggesting inadequate AED therapy rather than wrong diagnosis. Each red herring is realistic and would genuinely mislead a less experienced clinician.

educational_value.notes: One of the best cases in the entire set. Teaches FBDS recognition, hippocampal swelling vs sclerosis, LGI1 encephalitis presentation, SIADH association, PET hypermetabolism in autoimmune encephalitis, and the critical distinction that AED-refractory seizures should prompt autoimmune workup. Every element is high-yield.

strengths: FBDS as pathognomonic for LGI1 is a must-know clinical pearl
Hippocampal swelling vs sclerosis distinction is brilliantly designed
Hyponatremia as a diagnostic clue for LGI1 is well-integrated
PET hypermetabolism vs hypometabolism teaching point is critical
Red herrings are cleverly constructed and clinically realistic
Comprehensive workup including CT body for paraneoplastic screening
